food_subgroup: Noodle dish with grilled meat and broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bun Cha. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (composite noodle dish with meat) is the closest parent. No specific Bun Cha entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Bun Cha or a sufficiently close Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bun Cha is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli noodles, several pieces of grilled pork (patties and/or belly slices), and a side bowl of dipping broth with herbs and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Cha — composite nutrient estimate based on USDA FoodData Central component data: rice vermicelli (USDA #20065), grilled pork (USDA #10219), fish sauce (USDA #16112), and fresh herbs
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Cha as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for rice vermicelli (cooked), grilled pork (loin/belly), fish sauce, and fresh herbs, weighted by typical recipe proportions: ~40% rice vermicelli cooked, ~35% grilled pork, ~15% dipping broth, ~10% fresh herbs and vegetables. Per-100g values represent the blended dish average.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Cha as a composite dish. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked rice vermicelli, which has a published GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. The protein and fat content of the grilled pork and broth components further attenuate the postprandial glycemic response, making GI 53 a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 66.6) / 100 ≈ 35.3, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 14.8 × 4.5 = 66.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dipping broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bun Cha consumption or request reduced fish sauce in the broth, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium — exceeding 80% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving of Bun Cha delivers approximately 66 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~35, which is moderate-to-high; portion control of the rice vermicelli component and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled pork and elevated sodium from fish sauce broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate Bun Cha intake due to its saturated fat contribution from pork (approximately 5.4 g per serving) and its high sodium load from fish sauce, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Cha (Bún chả) is a classic North Vietnamese noodle dish originating from Hanoi, Vietnam. It consists of three main components: thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún), charcoal-grilled pork — typically a combination of seasoned pork patties (chả viên) and grilled pork belly slices (chả miếng) — and a warm, sweet-savoury dipping broth (nước chấm pha) made from diluted fish sauce, sugar, vinegar or lime juice, garlic, and chilli. The dish is served with a generous plate of fresh herbs including mint, perilla (tía tô), lettuce, and sometimes green papaya or carrot pickles. Diners dip the noodles and pork into the broth rather than eating it as a soup. The flavour profile is a balance of smoky, savoury, sweet, sour, and fresh herbal notes. Texturally, the dish contrasts soft, slippery noodles with charred, slightly crispy pork. Bun Cha is a moderate-calorie dish (~118 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution from the grilled pork (~8.2 g/100 g) and a moderate carbohydrate load from the rice vermicelli (~14.8 g/100 g). The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) derived from fish sauce, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), driven by the rice vermicelli base, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~35). In Singapore, Bun Cha is served at Vietnamese restaurants and is popular as a lunch or dinner option. It is gluten-free and dairy-free but is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those observing halal dietary requirements due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Bun Cha as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for the constituent ingredients weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the recipe proportions are well-documented in culinary literature.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no direct composite dish entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA for Bun Cha. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this dish composition. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this dish composition. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported across all component sources with sufficient confidence to aggregate. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in grilled lean pork and rice vermicelli per USDA data; confirmed zero by component source data. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source; the protein and fat from pork further attenuate glycemic response. 7. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal or kosher. 8. The food_name in the input ('VS4. Bun Cha 越南烤肉面') has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Bun Cha'. 9. Cuisine is classified as Vietnamese (origin: Hanoi) rather than Singaporean, as this is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming follows Vietnamese dish rules with additional Singaporean-context languages included for local market relevance. 10. Serving size of 450 g is based on typical restaurant portion sizes for Bun Cha in Singapore as observed in culinary references.
